coredata.dc:title: Catalytic ozonation performance of graphene quantum dot doped MnOOH nanorod for effective treatment of ciprofloxacin and bromate formation control in water
coredata.dc:creator: Le, Van-Re
Nguyen, Thanh-Binh
Chen, Chiu-Wen
Huang, C.P.
Bui, Xuan-Thanh
Dong, Cheng-Di
coredata.dc:description: A catalyst GQDs@MnOOH was successfully synthesized by attaching graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the surface of MnOOH nanorods to boost catalytic ozonation of antibiotic, exemplified by ciprofloxacin (CIP). The result demonstrated that the GQDs@MnOOH/O3 system had the greatest CIP removal effectiveness, followed by that of MnOOH/O3 and O3 only. The 0.02 mM CIP was degraded with 99.9% efficiency in 30 min in the presence 9.6 mg L-1 of O3 catalyzed by 12.5 mg L-1 of GQDs@MnOOH. The kinetic rate constants ​​were in the order: GQDs@MnOOH/O3 (0.161 min−1) > MnOOH/O3 (0.079 min−1) > O3 (0.055 min−1). The GQDs@MnOOH could enhance CIP degradation and inhibit BrO3 - formation in different water sources. Results of scavenger and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments demonstrated that oxygen radical (O2•-), singlet oxygen (1O2), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were involved in CIP degradation by the GQDs@MnOOH/O3 system. Accordingly, the degradation pathways of CIP and mechanism of catalytic ozonation over GQDs@MnOOH were investigated and proposed. This research is expected to shed light on the connection between carbonaceous material and metal hydroxide in catalytic ozonation.

(a) Adsorption of bromate (BrO3 -) and bromide (Br-) by GQDs@MnOOH, (b) BrO3 - formation and (c) CIP degradation with and without the presence of Br- during ozonation and catalytic ozonation, and (d) effect of pH on BrO3 - formation in catalytic ozonation system. Experimental conditions: [GQDs@MnOOH] = 12.5 mg L-1, [Br-] = 4 mg L-1, [O3] = 9.6 mg L-1, [CIP] = 0.02 mM, pH = 7, V = 400 mL.

(a) Scavengers test and (b) observed constant rate by methanol (MeOH), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), p-benzoquinone (p-Benzon), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). EPR spectra of (c and f) DMPO-●OH, (d and g) DMPO-O2 ●−, and TEMPO-1O2 achieved in single ozonation and catalytic ozonation. Experimental conditions: [Scavengers] = 1 mM, [O3] = 9.6 mg L-1, [GQDs@MnOOH] = 12.5 mg L-1, [CIP] = 0.02 mM, pH = 7, [V] = 400 mL, Temperature = 25 °C.
Mechanisms of catalytic ozonation activated by GQDs@MnOOH for CIP degradation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A catalyst GQDs@MnOOH was successfully synthesized by attaching graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the surface of MnOOH nanorods to boost catalytic ozonation of antibiotic, exemplified by ciprofloxacin (CIP). The result demonstrated that the GQDs@MnOOH/O3 system had the greatest CIP removal effectiveness, followed by that of MnOOH/O3 and O3 only. The 0.02 mM CIP was degraded with 99.9% efficiency in 30 min in the presence 9.6 mg L-1 of O3 catalyzed by 12.5 mg L-1 of GQDs@MnOOH. The kinetic rate constants ​​were in the order: GQDs@MnOOH/O3 (0.161 min−1) > MnOOH/O3 (0.079 min−1) > O3 (0.055 min−1). The GQDs@MnOOH could enhance CIP degradation and inhibit BrO3 - formation in different water sources. Results of scavenger and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments demonstrated that oxygen radical (O2•-), singlet oxygen (1O2), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were involved in CIP degradation by the GQDs@MnOOH/O3 system. Accordingly, the degradation pathways of CIP and mechanism of catalytic ozonation over GQDs@MnOOH were investigated and proposed. This research is expected to shed light on the connection between carbonaceous material and metal hydroxide in catalytic ozonation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Ozonation has been widely used in water purification, such as, disinfection of pathogens and decomposition of organic contaminants [1]. However, ozonation has some disadvantages such as low efficiency, inefficient mineralization of organic contaminants, and the generation of toxic disinfection by-products (DBPs) [2]. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been extensively studied and reported to be effective in the degradation of numerous organic contaminants because of highly reactive oxygen species (ROSs) such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), oxygen radical (O2 •-), and sulfate radical (SO4 •-) [3].

Recently, catalytic ozonation has become an alternative to conventional ozonation as to overcome the limitations of the later process [4,5]. Two modes of zonation are possible, i.e., direct oxidation by the O3 molecule and indirect oxidation by ROSs produced by the catalytic decomposition of O3 [6,7]. Homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis are the two main catalytic ozonation processes. Secondary contaminants, in the form of residual metallic ions, are byproducts of homogenous catalytic ozonation using metal ion catalysts [8]. In heterogeneous catalytic ozonation, metal oxide, metal oxides (often immobilized on supports), and carbon-based materials are common catalysts [7,9]. The disadvantages of homogenous catalytic ozonation have been ameliorated by the greater stability and efficiency of heterogeneous catalytic ozonation [10]. Metallic oxides such as aluminum oxides (
γ
-AlOOH,
α
-Al2O3, and
γ
-Al2O3), iron oxides (Fe2O3 and Fe3O4), iron oxyhydroxides (FeOOH), manganese oxides (MnO2), and bimetallic/polymetallic oxides (ZnAl2O4, CuFe2O4, CoFe2O4, and Mn-Ce-O composites) have been used mainly in heterogeneous catalytic ozonation. Among these metallic oxides, manganese-based materials are more appealing due to low cost, abundant mineral source, and high efficiency in catalytic ozonation with respect to decomposing organic pollutants [11,12]. Previous research has revealed that 1D manganite (MnOOH) possesses a high surface-active site density, an abundance of surface hydroxyl sites, a mobile lattice oxygen-rich structure, and a fast electron transfer capability. However, research on MnOOH for catalytic zonation of organic pollutants is limited.

Due to the fast reaction rates and environmentally friendly properties of carbocatalysis, the last two decades have seen the fast expansion of carbon-based materials as an alternative to conventional metal-based approaches for environmental remediation. Subsequently, a broad range of carbonaceous materials has found applications in heterogeneous catalytic ozonation. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a revolutionary generation of the carbon family that possesses both the exceptional capabilities of graphene and quantum dots, are acquiring a great deal of popularity due to their exceptional chemical stability, biocompatibility, and catalytic activity [13,14]. Studies have found that the structural defects and functional groups on GQDs created by different synthesis processes exhibit great effects on the generation of ROSs [15]. The application of 0D GQD-doped 1D MnOOH in the catalytic ozonation of antibiotic compounds has, to the best of our knowledge, not been investigated despite the aforementioned advantages and peculiar structural properties.

The present study was, (i) to propose a simple method for the synthesis of GQD@MnOOH, (ii) to elucidate the physicochemical properties of the prepared catalysts, (iii) to explore the catalytic ozonation preformation of the catalysts toward CIP degradation and parameters governing the oxidation process, and (iv) to determine the reaction mechanism and CIP degradation pathways.

The GQDs catalyst was synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid [13]. Briefly, 3.2 g citric acid was dissolved in 50 mL of DI water and sonicated for 30 min in an ultrasonicator. Then the solution was transferred to a 100-mL Teflon container and hydrothermally treated at 160 °C for 4 h. The treated solution was centrifuged to remove large particles and washed with anhydrous ethanol to remove residual water. The resultant solution was then dialyzed with dialysis membrane Spectra/Por® Biotech (MWCO 500–1000) and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The MnOOH was synthesized by hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 742.6 mg KMnO4 was dissolved in a mixture of 50 mL of DI water and 30 mL of ethanol at 20 °C for 30 min. The solution was transferred into a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated at 140 °C for 24 h, and then cooled naturally to room temperature. The precipitate was centrifuged and washed several times with DI water and anhydrous ethanol, then finally dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. GQDs@MnOOH was prepared by placing 200 mg of MnOOH in GQDs solution for 2 h for the successful attachment of GQDs on the former. The samples were washed in DI water and freeze-dried for 24 h.

Ciprofloxacin was chosen to study the conventional and catalytic ozonation process. All ozonation experiments were carried out in volumetric flasks (500 mL) with a magnetic stir to arrive at homogeneous state. The temperature of reactor was controlled and maintained by a refrigerated circulating constant bath through a jacket outside the reactor (±0.1 °C). Ozone was generated by the ozone generator (SETON, Taiwan) and bubbled into the CIP solution with an air bubble stone. The ozone flow rate was controlled in the range of 20 to 100 mL min−1 to obtain an ozone concentration of 0.6 to 28.8 mg L-1, respectively. The dissolved ozone concentration was monitored by the potassium iodide method.

In conventional ozonation experiments, ozone was bubbled into 400 mL CIP (0.02 mM) solution for 30 min. Samples were collected at specific time using a 1-mL syringe and filtered through 0.25-µm PVDF disc filter to collect the filtrate for analyzing the residual CIP concentration using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). After 30 min reaction, the sample was collected to measure pH and TOC. In catalytic ozonation experiments, a specific amount of catalyst was dissolved into 400 mL CIP (0.02 mM) by sonication for 1 min then bubbled with ozone following the same process as above.

The morphology of bare MnOOH and GQDs@MnOOH were observed by scanning electron Microscopes (SEM) and Transmittance Electron Microscopy (TEM). The bare MnOOH exhibited nanorod structure (Fig. 1 a). Typical length and diameter were around 5.50 µm and 77 nm, respectively (Fig. S1a, Fig. S1b). It must be noted that the morphology of MnOOH remained strongly intact without disintegration when being coated with GQDs onto its surface (Fig. 1d and 1e). Clusters of GQDs were immobilized on the surface of MnOOH nanorods, as seen in the HRTEM images (Fig. 1f and 1 g). Both MnOOH and GQDs exhibited a high degree of crystallinity, with lattice spacing of 0.24 and 0.21 nm, respectively, corresponding to (111) and (100) planes (Fig. 1h).

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm and pore size distribution of MnOOH and GQDs@MnOOH were shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b, respectively. MnOOH and GQDs@MnOOH were classified as type IV isotherm, which signified mesoporous structure. MnOOH and GQDs@MnOOH had specific surface area of 9.16 and 11.68 m2 g−1, respectively. Composite catalysts with a higher specific surface area may have greater catalytic capability. The pore size of MnOOH was primarily between 1.95 and 3.12 nm, whereas that of GQDs@MnOOH was primarily between 2.09 and 3.26 nm, exhibiting the well-established micro and mesoporous structure after GQDs coating on MnOOH.

The Fourier transformation infrared (FTIR) spectra revealed the surface functional groups (Fig. 2c). For MnOOH, two broad absorption bands at 1159 and 1082 cm−1 were assigned the typical O–H bending modes of –OH and –OH, respectively. The Mn-O vibration in γ-MnOOH was linked to three broad absorption bands ranging from 448 to 580 cm−1 [16,17]. The O–H stretching was assigned to two high broad bands at 2703 and 2083 cm−1 [18]. The characteristic absorption band of GQDs at 2069 and 1693 cm−1 corresponded to C = C and C = O stretching, respectively [13]. The bending vibrations of C-O were responsible for the major peak around 1360 cm−1. There were no discernible alternate bands in the composite with respect to its component catalysts,

The crystalline structure of prepared catalysts was investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 2d). In case of pure MnOOH, all of the major diffraction peaks (2θ = 26, 28.9, 32.2, and 36°) were in good agreement with those of monoclinic manganite (γ-MnOOH, JCPDS No. 41–1379). The GQD displayed a broad peak at 25.6°, which indicated the presence of (002) plane of graphite. With only a small amount of GQD doping, the primary MnOOH peaks were unaffected, and no additional peaks of GQD were visible in the composite.

CIP degradation experiments, including adsorption, ozonation, and catalytic ozonation over MnOOH and GQDs@MnOOH were carried out to assess the efficacy of the catalytic ozonation process (Fig. 3 a). Under the present experimental conditions, CIP was minimally adsorbed by MnOOH (7%) and GQDs@MnOOH (13%) after 30 min. Ozonation alone increased CIP degradation efficiency up to 35% while the extent of mineralization was only 20.1% after 30 min. Meanwhile, the presence of GQDs@MnOOH in the catalytic ozonation process significantly enhanced degradation and TOC removal efficiency compared to that of ozonation-only process. CIP and TOC removal efficiencies were 99.9% and 90.1%, respectively, after 30 min of catalytic ozonation over GQDs@MnOOH. In comparison, only 85% of CIP degradation and 54.4% of mineralization were attained after 30 min of ozonation over MnOOH. The results showed that GQDs@MnOOH enhanced CIP degradation and was more effective in catalyzing the ozonation of CIP than that without MnOOH support, i.e., GQDs alone. The decoration of GQDs on the surface of 1D MnOOH increased the specific surface area and number of active sites of the composite catalyst that accelerated the generation of ROSs in the catalytic ozonation system, thereby increasing the oxidative capacity of the process.

The pseudo-first-order kinetic equation quantitatively described well CIP degradation by catalytic ozonation as follows [19]:
(1)
C t

where kobs is the observed kinetic rate constant, C0 is the initial CIP concentration and Ct is the residual CIP concentration at a specific time (t). Fig. 3b gives the calculated kobs The kobs followed the order: GQDs@MnOOH/O3 (0.161 min−1) > MnOOH/O3 (0.079x10-1 min−1) > O3 (0.055 min−1). The findings showed that heterogeneous catalytic ozonation of CIP over GQDs@MnOOH was more efficient than the ozonation-only process. In addition, surface modification of MnOOH by GQDs could facilitate charge transport from the CIP to oxidants in the surface region of the carbocatalyst to destruct the contaminant in question.

More importantly, electrochemical measurements were performed to assess the catalytic reactivity in terms of electron mitigation during catalytic ozonation of CIP in a solution of 0.2 M Na2SO4. According to the Tafel polarization curve (Fig. 3c), the corrosion potential of MnOOH was −0.63 V vs. Ag/AgCl, whereas GQDs@MnOOH had a more negative corrosion potential (-0.76 V vs. Ag/AgCl). The relatively higher corrosion current of GQDs@MnOOH than that of bare MnOOH was indicative of the composite being more superior in electron transfer and catalytic reactivity with respect to its parent materials. Meanwhile, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) of GQDs@MnOOH (Fig. 3d) presented intensified area of cyclic voltammetry spectrum, and obvious reversible redox processes of Mn(II)/Mn(III) occurring at the surface of the composite during the catalytic decomposition of O3, which further confirmed the enhanced interfacial electron transfer ability for faster ROSs generation and higher catalytic activity of GQDs@MnOOH.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to investigate the charge transfer resistance of catalysts. In the region of the Nyquist plot, the semicircle diameter of GQDs@MnOOH was smaller than that of MnOOH, which indicated lower electron transfer resistance that facilitated high-efficiency charge transfer for catalytic ozonation (Fig. 3e). In addition, chronoamperometry analysis was run on both electrodes (Fig. 3f) as to assess the electron transfer characteristics. Results showed current jump upon spiking CIP to the electrolyte solution, which suggested electron transfer occur at the electrode-solution interface. In particular, the magnitude of the current jump correlated well with the catalytic ozonation capacities of the materials, which was increased with the modification of MnOOH with GQDs. The results of electrochemical characterization of GQDs@MnOOH demonstrated excellent electron transfer capacity toward facilitating catalytic ozonation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of operating parameters on CIP degradation

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a. shows the effect of ozone concentration on CIP degradation. The CIP removal efficiency increased from 23 to 99% in 30 min with increase in ozone dosage from 0.6 to 28.8 mg L-1, respectively. The production of •OH enhanced the degradation of organic contaminants at high ozone concentrations [20]. However, high ozone concentration (i.e., 9.6–28.8 mg L-1) did not increase CIP oxidation further, which could be attributed to the self-scavenging effect of ROSs. Fig. 4b. gives the observed kinetic rate constants as a function of ozone dosage. The kobs was 0.239 min−1 at 28.8 mg L-1 of ozone, which was 1.1, 1.5, 9.2, and 23.9 times that at 20.4, 9.6, 4.8, and 0.6 mg L-1, respectively. Considering both CIP removal efficiency and material chemical cost, the optimal O3 dosage will be 9.6 mg L-1.

Fig. 4c shows the effect of GQDs@MnOOH dosage on CIP degradation at constant ozone dose of 9.6 mg L-1. The CIP removal efficiency was>98% in 30 min when the GQDs@MnOOH dose was between 5 and 12.5 mg L-1. As shown in Fig. 4d, the rate constant and TOC removal efficiency increased abruptly from that of control to 5 to 12.5 mg L-1, which could be attributed to the increase in catalytically active sites [21]. However, the reaction rate and TOC removal efficiency decreased slowly and gradually when the catalyst concentration was increased from 12.5 to 200 mg L-1. Obviously, excess catalyst could scavenge ROSs. Further experiments were carried out at the optimal GQDs@MnOOH dosage of 12.5 mg L-1.

The pH is an important parameter controlling the catalytic ozonation processes because of direct and indirect reactions affecting electrophilic attack by molecular ozone or ozone decomposition, which generates abundant reactive oxidizing species that impact electrostatic interaction between catalyst and pollutant [22]. Fig. 5 a shows the effect of pH on CIP degradation. The results showed that CIP degradation was high at pH range of 5–9 (>99%). CIP removal effectiveness was slightly lower at pH 3 and pH 11 (88% and 90%, respectively). The electrostatic force between catalyst and ciprofloxacin was responsible for relatively constant CIP degradation between pH 3 and pH 11. Since the surface charge of catalyst was negative while CIP was mainly positively charged, the electrostatic attractive force between the catalyst and ciprofloxacin was rather strong in the pH range of 5 to pH 9, (Fig. S2a and S2b). Note that the surface charge of the catalyst and CIP was similarly negative (pH 11) and positive (pH 3). Furthermore, pH also affected the solubility of ozone in aqueous solution. The ozone concentration was shown at various solution pH levels (Fig. S3a). Since increase in solution pH marginally decreased ozone concentration. The CIP removal efficiency was low because of low ozone concentration at pH 11. At low pH, molecular ozone is the major oxidant, whereas at alkaline pH, ozone is transformed to reactive oxidation species such as ●OH, HO2 ●, and O2 ●− [23]. However, at high pH, ●OH can be deprotonated to O●− which has low oxidation capacity (Eq. 2.) [23]. Besides, initial conditions with a lower pH resulted in a greater amount of released Mn, which in turn reduced the number of surface catalytic sites ( Fig. S4 ).

Fig. 5b shows the observed kinetics rate constant of CIP and TOC removal efficiency at different pH levels. The observed kinetics rate constants were 0.161 min−1 (pH 7) > 0.147 min−1 (pH 5) > 0.130 min−1 (pH 9) > 0.099 min−1 (pH 11) > 0.081 min−1 (pH 3). The TOC removal efficiency remained high in the range of 71.5 to 90.1% when pH was increased from pH 3 to pH 7, then declined to 77.1% at pH 11.

Fig. 5c demonstrated the effect of temperature on CIP degradation. The CIP degradation increased significantly from 65.5 to 99% with temperature being increased from 5 to 25 °C then slightly decreased to 88.2% at 30 °C. The results indicated that the highest CIP degradation occurred at 25 °C with 99% removal. The results reflected the state of ozone concentration (Fig. S3b). Elevated temperature, i.e., from 5 to 25 °C, enhanced ozone decomposition and mass transfer in solution. Note that ozone concentration was increased significantly in 2.5 min at 5–25 °C to the highest level in 30 min at 25 °C. At low temperatures molecular ozone was the dominant species, therefore CIP oxidation occurred in direct ozonation mode, which is not as efficient as indirect ozonation when ROS is responsible for CIP oxidation. Noticeably, further increase in temperature up to 30 °C hampered ozone solubility. Therefore, a 15-min reaction time at 30 °C yielded the lowest ozone concentration. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, the highest TOC removal efficiency was 90% at 25 °C followed by 82, 71, and 44% at 30, 25, and 5 °C, respectively. The observed kinetics rate constant was 0.161 min−1 at 25 °C, which was>2, 3.5, and 4.4 times that at 30, 25, and 5 °C, respectively.

Since ozone concentration at 15 °C was close to that at 25 °C, an attempt was made to analyze the rate theory according to the Arrhenius equation using data obtained at these three temperature points, i.e., 15, 20 and 25 °C, accordingly:
(3)
k = A exp -
E a
RT

or
(3a)
log k = log A -
E a
RT

A plot of log k versus
1 T
yields intercept and slope, from which the frequency factor, A, and the activation energy, Ea, can be obtained ( Fig. S5 ). Results gave 8.24x1014 min−1 and 38.9 kJ mol−1, for A and Ea, respectively. The kinetics of catalytic ozonation of CIP was limited by chemical reaction because of the large activation energy.

The effect of foreign cations and anions on CIP degradation by catalytic ozonation process was conducted. In the presence of inorganic ions, the charge of the catalyst remained negatively charged (Fig. S2c and S2d), favoring attraction force with CIP (dominant positive charge) at pH 7. As a result, the effect of inorganic ions via adsorption may be negligible. CIP degradation and TOC removal efficiency decreased slightly after 30 min reaction in the presence of anions (Fig. 6 a). The kobs in the presence of SO4 2-, Cl-, NO3 –, HCO3 – were 0.08, 0.11, 0.11, and 0.10 min−1, respectively (Fig. 6b). This observation can be explained by anions that being function as ROS scavengers, which may interact with the radical chain reactions that lead to reduce the ROSs and O3 concentration, as stated by the following equations (Eq. 4–8) [24,25]. Moreover, ozone dissolution decreased with the presence of anions, leading to a lower CIIP degradation efficiency ( Fig. S6 ) [26]. Noticeably, the negative effect of divalent SO4 2- anion was more significant than that of the others, which is in good agreement with the results of CIP degradation.
Cl− + ●OH → ClOH●− (4).
HCO3 − + ●OH → HCO3 ●− + OH− (6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of bromate formation in catalytic ozonation over GQDs@MnOOH
ce:section.ce:para: Bromide (Br-) is frequently present in water and wastewater sources. Moreover, bromate (BrO3 -) has been identified as carcinogen formed in the ozonation or ozone-based advanced oxidation processes through chain reactions with O3 and ●OH when Br- concentration in water exceeds 50 µg L-1 [27]. The effect of Br- on the ozonation of CIP and the subsequent formation of BrO3 - with and without GQDs@MnOOH was investigated. Results of Fig. 7 a showed no adsorption of both BrO3 - and Br- by GQDs@MnOOH in 30 min. Therefore, the transformation of Br- to BrO3 - mainly occurred in the aqueous phase. Fig. 7b gives result on the formation of BrO3 - and change of Br- concentration during catalytic ozonation of CIP over GQDs@MnOOH. The concentration of BrO3 - increased significantly with the reaction time, reaching 1.41 and 0.9 mg L-1 in the absence and presence of GODs@MnOOH, respectively, in 30 min. Meanwhile, the concentration of Br- decreased rapidly, from 4.0 to 3.6 and 2.6 mg L-1, respectively, with and without GQDs@MnOOH. Results demonstrated that GQDs@MnOOH suppressed BrO3 - formation during catalytic ozonation of CIP. BrO3 - formation occurred because of the oxidation of Br- by either O3 or ●OH radical. O3 decomposition was enhanced in the presence of catalysts, thereby limiting the bromate formation [28]. Therefore, catalytic ozonation of CIP did not have adverse effect with respect to bromate formation (Fig. 7c). Besides, GQDs@MnOOH promoted the formation of ●OH radicals, and the reaction between ●OH radical and CIP was faster than that with Br- to form BrO3 -. The above results demonstrated that GQDs@MnOOH enhanced CIP removal efficiency while effectively minimized BrO3 - formation in the catalytic ozonation process.

Fig. 7d shows the effect of pH on bromate formation in the ozonation process. Results indicates that the BrO3 - concentration increase from 0.686 to 1,743 mg L-1 when increasing the pH from 3 to 11, respectively. Meanwhile, BrO3 - formation decreased with increasing initial pH in the catalytic ozonation system. At high pH, the increase in BrO3 - concentration was due to the presence of abundant OBr- (pKa = 8.8–9.0, HOBr/OBr-). Lower pH may shift the HOBr/OBr- equilibrium toward HOBr and decrease the molar ratio of ●OH to O3, thus directly reducing the BrO3 - formation [29]. Additionally, O3 reacts with OBr- more readily (100 M−1 s−1) compared to HOBr (0.013 M−1 s−1). The above results indicated that the catalyst is capable of minimizing the production of BrO3 - at various pH levels.

The degradation of other organic pollutants, such as Ofloxacin (OFL), Norfloxacin (NOR), Rhodamine B (RhB), and methyl orange (MO), was examined to evaluate the selectivity in catalytic ozonation of antibiotics over GQDs@MnOOH. The results showed high decomposition efficiency (95%) for all pollutants studied. The TOC removal efficiency was 81.5, 88.2, 86.8, and 90.6% for OFL, NOR, MO, and RhB, respectively after 30 min of reaction in catalytic ozonation system (Fig. S8a and S8b). The kobs follow the order: RhB (0.244 min−1) > MO (0.156 min−1) > OFL (0.149 min−1) > NOR (0.145 min−1) (Fig. S8b). In brief, GQDs@MnOOH showed high catalytic ozonation efficiency in the oxidation of a variety of contaminants.

The possibility of applying GQDs@MnOOH for catalytic ozonation to remove organic contaminants from various natural water resources, including tap water (TW) and river water (RW) (Hojing River, Taiwan), was examined. After 30 min of reaction, the CIP removal effectiveness in TW and RW was marginally lower than that in deionized water under catalytic ozonation. The kobs followed the order: DIW (0.16 min−1) > TW (0.13 min−1) > RW (0.098 min−1). TOC removal efficiency was 90.1%, 75.81, and 35.82% for DIW, TW, and RW, respectively (Fig. S7c and S7d). The major chemical composition of various natural water resources studied was presented ( Table S2
). The presence of inorganic ions and natural organic matters tended to compete with ozone and ROSs for reaction with CIP, which explained the reason that CIP degradation and TOC removal efficiency in RW and TW was lower than that in DIW. In addition, the BrO3 - formation was determined during the degradation of CIP in different water sources. No significant difference in BrO3 - formation was found in the three water samples, indicating that the catalytic ozonation over MnOOH@GQD inhibited BrO3 - formation during the catalytic ozonation of pollutants in natural water sources (Fig. S9a and S9b).

To elucidate the mechanism of CIP degradation via catalytic ozonation over GQDs@MnOOH, the scavenger test using methanol (MeOH), Furfuryl alcohol (FFA), p-benzoquinone (p-Benzon), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), identifiers for ●OH radicals, singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide radicals (O2 ●−) and surface-bound active radicals, respectively, was conducted (Fig. 8 a and 8b). Normaly, methanol has been used to quench hydroxyl (●OH) radical (9.7x108 M−1 s−1). After 30 min of reaction with methanol (1 mM), the CIP degradation rate dropped from 99 to 57.5%, indicating the significant role of ●OH in catalytic ozonation. Meanwhile, p-benzoquinone is able to quench O2 ●− (9.6x108 M−1 s−1) [30,31]. As a result, the removal efficiency of CIP decreased rapidly from 99 to 79.9% in the presence of p-benzoquinone, implying O2 ●− contribution to CIP degradation. FFA is capable of scavenging 1O2 (1.2 x108 M−1 s−1). FFA reduced CIP removal significantly more than MeOH and p-Benzon, indicating high degree contribution of 1O2 to CIP degradation. However, the effect of FFA on CIP degradation, however, only suggests the potential role of 1O2 in CIP degradation because FFA also reacts with ●OH radical at a rate comparable to 1O2 [32]. The degradation of CIP was only 27.8% in 30 min in the presence of DMSO, indicating the important role of surface-bound active radicals. Thus, the accumulation of CIP or O3 on the GQDs@MnOOH surface played a crucial role in CIP degradation during catalytic ozonation. The kobs of scavenger test followed the order: without scavenger (0.161 min−1) > p-Benzon (0.069 min−1) > MeOH (0.055 min−1) > FFA (0.02 min−1) > DMSO (0.011 min−1) (Fig. 8b). It can also be found that the removal efficiency of CIP with scavengers were even less than the single ozonation ( Fig. S10
). It might attribute to the competition for O3 and ROS by excessive scavengers and their intermediates.

To verify the possible existence of ROS, electronic paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis was conducted using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMPO) trapping agents. In both simple and catalytic ozonation, four main peaks with a height ratio of 1:2:2:1 were detected with hyperfine splitting constants of αN = αH = 14.9 G and g-value = 2.0067 (Fig. 8c and 8f) [33,34]. Furthermore, the high peak intensity of DMPO-●OH in the GQDs@MnOOH/O3 system was higher than that in the simple ozonation, showing that more ●OH was generated in catalytic ozonation. For the detection of O2 ●−, methanol solution was used as reaction matrix instead of DI water to inhibit the disproportionation of O2 ●−. Four-peak spectrum with the characteristics intensity ratio of 1:1:1: was assigned to DMPO-O2 ●− in both systems (Fig. 8d and 8 g), suggesting the possible involvement of O2 ●− in CIP oxidation [35]. However, the peak intensity of DMPO-O2 ●− was lower than that of DMPO-●OH since O2 ●− is an intermediate radical of a radical chain process during O3 decomposition and ●OH generation [36]. From the EPR spectra (Fig. 8e and f), both systems showed the same TEMPO-1O2 adduct signals with equal intensities (αN = 7.2 G and αH = 4.1 G), and the intensity signal increased significantly with the addition of GQDs@MnOOH. According to the results of the scavenger and EPR test, 1O2 was the dominant ROS responsible for CIP degradation and the catalyst aided in the conversion of O3 to O2 ●− and ●OH while also promoting the formation of 1O2. Moreover, CIP degradation experiment in deuterium oxide (D2O) system is conducted ( Fig. S11 ). Since 1O2 has a longer lifetime in D2O than in water, it may be able to react with CIP more effectively in D2O than in water [37]. Degradation of CIP was found to be faster in D2O than in water, demonstrating the predominant role of 1O2 in this process.

Based on the above consequences, a reaction mechanism of catalytic ozonation activated by GQDs@MnOOH for CIP degradation was proposed (Fig. 9 ). The heterogeneous catalytic ozonation for the degradation of organic pollutant involved several pathways on the decomposition of O3 to generate ●OH via redox reaction involving Mn ions (Eq. 8 and 9) [38–40]. The regeneration of reduced metal ions occurred via the oxidation with O2 ●− and HO2 ● (Eq. 10 and 11), which were formed during O3 decomposition (Eq. 12–18) [40]. Besides, 1O2 could be formed through the reaction between O2 ●− and H+ ions according to Eq. 19 [41]. The surface of heterogeneous catalyst was covered with various functional groups, such as surface hydroxyl groups (–OH), which are the main adsorption centers of the catalyst, as well as some Brönsted and Lewis acid sites, which may be the catalytic centers and can adsorb some Lewis bases, such as H2O and O3 [42]. Furthermore, the –OH groups of MnOOH or GQDs would react with molecular ozone to form ●OH and O2 ●− (Eq. 20–22) [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed degradation pathway of catalytic CIP ozonation
ce:section.ce:para:
LC-MS was used to study the intermediates and degradation pathways of CIP oxidation in the GQDs@MnOOH/O3 system. Furthermore, to identify the active sites responsible for CIP degradation, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), electrostatic potential (ESP) mapped molecular surface, and Fukui index distribution were calculated using DFT at the B3LYP/6–31 + G level (d) ( Fig. S12 and Table S3 ). The ESP distribution of CIP demonstrated the highly negative charge density region (2O, 3O, 4O, and 7 N) and positive charge (C16, C20, and cyclopropane group) that were more easily attacked by O3, and anionic ROSs as ●OH, respectively ( Fig. S12
b) [43]. In the HOMO and LUMO distribution of CIP, green and red indicate poor electron and rich electron zones, respectively. Additionally, the electron from HOMO states tends to transform to LUMO states, making it susceptible to be attacked by ROSs ( Fig. S12
c and d) [44]. The Fukui function or frontier molecular orbital function describes the electron density in a boundary orbital with high values of f0 and f+, f− being more susceptible to radical, nucleophilic, and electrophilic reactions, respectively. [45,46]. DFT calculations revealed that the positions of O2, H33, N6, C12, and C19 have a higher Fukui index value than other sites, so these sites were more vulnerable to attack. On the basis of LC-MS results, seven byproducts were identified, and four CIP degradation pathways were proposed. The proposed degradation pathways of catalytic CIP ozonation were displayed schematically in Fig. 10 . In degradation pathway I, the CIP structure was attacked by O2 ●− and ●OH at the Fluorine site yielding the defluorination product P1 (m/z = 330) [47]. The ROSs attack on the piperazine ring of CIP in pathway II, split the piperazine ring with the formation of the P2 intermediate. Subsequently, P2 was converted to intermediate P3 by substituting the –NH2 with double-bonded oxygen. In the degradation pathway III, the destruction of quinolones can be confronted by ●OH on the carbon–carbon double bond adjacent to the carboxylic acid group [48]. As a result, quinolone moieties of CIP were converted to intermediate P4 (m/z = 363) and then further transformed to P5 (m/z = 335) via –CO group loss. The piperazine ring of intermediate P5 was continuously cleaved to intermediate P6 (m/z = 290). In pathway IV, the carboxylic group was removed from the CIP structure while two more ester groups were added to the piperazine ring to form intermediate P7 (m/z = 340). The intermediates would be broken to even smaller intermediates as the reaction proceeded, and eventually produced H2O and CO2 as final products.

A catalyst GQDs@MnOOH was successfully synthesized by coating GQDs on the surface of MnOOH nanorods to enhance catalytic ozonation toward the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP). The results demonstrated that the GQDs@MnOOH/O3 system exhibit the highest CIP removal efficiency, followed by the MnOOH/O3 system and conventional ozonation. The degradation of CIP (0.02 mM) was 99.9% in 30 min in the presence of 12.5 mg L-1 of GQDs@MnOOH, and 9.6 mg L-1 of O3. The observed kinetics rate constant ​​followed the order: GQDs@MnOOH/O3 (0.161 min−1) > MnOOH/O3 (0.079 min−1) > O3 (0.055 min−1). Results of scavengers and electron paramagnetic resonance experiments demonstrated that singlet oxygen (1O2), oxygen radical (O2•-), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were involved in CIP degradation by the GQDs@MnOOH/O3 system. Besides, GQDs@MnOOH promoted the formation of ●OH radicals, and the reaction between ●OH radical and CIP was faster than that with Br- to form BrO3 -. This research presents a novel opportunity to develop a cost-effective and efficient catalytic ozonation catalyst and a potential approach for treating antibiotic-containing wastewater.

Van-Re Le: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Thanh-Binh Nguyen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chiu-Wen Chen: Resources. C.P. Huang: Writing – review & editing. Xuan-Thanh Bui: Writing – review & editing. Cheng-Di Dong: Resources, Funding acquisition.
